title: Dead Sea Scrolls
description: A collection of nearly 1,000 manuscripts discovered in caves near the Dead Sea. They include the oldest known copies of Hebrew Bible texts, predating previously known manuscripts by roughly 1,000 years.
current_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem
significance: Confirmed the remarkable textual accuracy of the Hebrew Bible over a millennium of transmission. Contains fragments of every Old Testament book except Esther.

title: Rosetta Stone
description: A granodiorite stele inscribed with a decree in three scripts: Egyptian hieroglyphics, Demotic, and Ancient Greek. Its discovery enabled the decipherment of Egyptian hieroglyphics.
significance: Unlocked the ability to read ancient Egyptian texts, providing crucial context for understanding the biblical narrative of the Exodus and Israel's time in Egypt.

title: Tel Dan Inscription
description: A fragmentary Aramaic stele discovered at Tel Dan in northern Israel. It contains the phrase 'House of David' — the first mention of King David found outside the Bible.
significance: Provides the earliest extra-biblical evidence for the existence of King David and his royal dynasty, corroborating the biblical accounts in Samuel and Kings.

title: Cyrus Cylinder
description: An ancient clay cylinder inscribed in Akkadian cuneiform by Cyrus the Great of Persia, recording his conquest of Babylon and his policy of returning displaced peoples to their homelands.
significance: Corroborates the biblical account in Ezra and 2 Chronicles of Cyrus issuing a decree allowing the Jewish exiles to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple.

title: Pilate Inscription
description: A limestone block bearing a dedicatory inscription from Pontius Pilate, identifying him as prefect of Judaea. Found in the ruins of a Roman theater at Caesarea Maritima.
significance: Provides the only known archaeological evidence for the existence of Pontius Pilate, confirming the Gospel accounts of the Roman governor who presided over the trial of Jesus.

title: James Ossuary
description: A first-century limestone bone box bearing the Aramaic inscription 'James, son of Joseph, brother of Jesus.' Its authenticity has been debated by scholars.
significance: If authentic, this is the earliest artifact directly referencing Jesus of Nazareth by name, connecting to the New Testament figure of James, leader of the Jerusalem church.

title: Hezekiah's Tunnel (Siloam Tunnel)
description: A 533-meter underground water tunnel carved through solid rock beneath Jerusalem during the reign of King Hezekiah, diverting the Gihon Spring inside the city walls.
significance: Directly confirms the account in 2 Kings 20:20 and 2 Chronicles 32:30 of Hezekiah's water engineering project to prepare Jerusalem for the Assyrian siege.

title: Babylonian Chronicles
description: A series of clay tablets recording major events of Babylonian history, including Nebuchadnezzar II's capture of Jerusalem in 597 BC.
significance: Provides independent confirmation of the biblical account of the Babylonian conquest of Jerusalem and the exile of King Jehoiachin, as recorded in 2 Kings 24.

title: Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele)
description: A basalt stele erected by King Mesha of Moab around 840 BC, recording his victories over Israel. It mentions the Israelite God YHWH and the 'House of David.'
significance: Corroborates biblical accounts in 2 Kings 3 of the conflict between Moab and Israel. Contains one of the earliest references to YHWH outside the Bible.

title: Merneptah Stele
description: An Egyptian victory stele from Pharaoh Merneptah (c. 1208 BC) containing the earliest known extra-biblical reference to 'Israel' as a people in Canaan.
significance: Provides the oldest evidence outside the Bible for the existence of Israel as an identifiable people, dating to the period of the Judges.

title: Codex Sinaiticus
description: One of the oldest and most complete manuscripts of the Christian Bible, written in Greek on parchment in the 4th century AD. Contains the entire New Testament.
significance: One of the two most important witnesses to the Greek text of the New Testament. Essential for textual criticism and establishing the reliability of biblical manuscripts.

title: Codex Vaticanus
description: A 4th-century Greek manuscript of the Bible, considered one of the most important witnesses to the original text. Contains most of the Old and New Testaments.
significance: Along with Codex Sinaiticus, forms the foundation for modern critical editions of the Greek New Testament. Its careful scribal tradition indicates early concern for accurate transmission.

title: Ketef Hinnom Scrolls
description: Two tiny silver scrolls containing the oldest known text from the Hebrew Bible — the Priestly Blessing from Numbers 6:24-26, dating to the 7th century BC.
significance: Predates the Dead Sea Scrolls by 400 years, providing the oldest known fragments of biblical text and confirming the antiquity of the Priestly Blessing.

title: Lachish Letters
description: A collection of ostraca (inscribed pottery shards) found at Tell ed-Duweir, ancient Lachish. Written in Hebrew, they date to the last days before Nebuchadnezzar's conquest.
current_location: British Museum, London / Israel Museum, Jerusalem
significance: Provide a firsthand account of the final days of Judah before the Babylonian destruction, corroborating the setting described in Jeremiah 34:7.

title: Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III
description: A Neo-Assyrian black limestone obelisk depicting King Jehu of Israel bowing before the Assyrian emperor Shalmaneser III, dating to 841 BC.
significance: Contains the only known contemporary depiction of an Israelite king. Confirms the existence of King Jehu, whose reign is described in 2 Kings 9-10.

title: Taylor Prism (Sennacherib's Prism)
description: A hexagonal clay prism containing Sennacherib's account of his military campaigns, including his siege of Jerusalem during King Hezekiah's reign.
significance: Confirms the biblical account of the Assyrian siege of Jerusalem (2 Kings 18-19, Isaiah 36-37). Sennacherib boasts of trapping Hezekiah 'like a caged bird' but notably never claims to have conquered Jerusalem.

title: Siloam Inscription
description: A Hebrew inscription carved into the wall of Hezekiah's Tunnel, describing how two teams of workers tunneling from opposite ends met in the middle.
significance: Provides a contemporary account of the construction of Hezekiah's Tunnel, confirming the engineering feat described in 2 Kings 20:20.

title: Sennacherib's Lachish Reliefs
description: Carved stone panels from Sennacherib's palace at Nineveh depicting the Assyrian siege and conquest of the Judean city of Lachish in 701 BC.
significance: Provide detailed visual evidence of the Assyrian campaign against Judah described in 2 Kings 18:13-14 and 2 Chronicles 32:9.

title: Nabonidus Chronicle
description: A Babylonian clay tablet recording the fall of Babylon to the Persian forces of Cyrus the Great in 539 BC without a battle.
significance: Corroborates the account in Daniel 5 of the fall of Babylon and provides historical context for the decree of Cyrus allowing the Jews to return to Jerusalem.

title: Pool of Siloam
description: A rock-cut pool at the southern end of Hezekiah's Tunnel in Jerusalem, identified as the Pool of Siloam where Jesus healed a blind man (John 9:7).
significance: Confirms the existence and location of the Pool of Siloam mentioned in the Gospel of John, validating the historical accuracy of the narrative.

title: Caiaphas Ossuary
description: An ornate limestone ossuary bearing the inscription 'Joseph son of Caiaphas,' believed to have held the remains of the high priest who presided over the trial of Jesus.
significance: Provides archaeological evidence for the historical existence of Caiaphas, the high priest described in the Gospel accounts of Jesus' trial (Matthew 26:57).

title: Pilate's Ring
description: A bronze ring discovered at the Herodium fortress bearing a Greek inscription reading 'of Pilatus,' potentially belonging to Pontius Pilate or someone in his administration.
significance: A second artifact bearing Pilate's name, supplementing the Pilate Inscription and further confirming the historical existence of the Roman governor mentioned in the Gospels.

title: Nazareth Inscription
description: A marble tablet with a Greek inscription issued by an unnamed Caesar (possibly Claudius), imposing the death penalty for disturbing graves or moving bodies.
significance: May reflect Roman awareness of early Christian claims about the resurrection of Jesus, suggesting the empty tomb narrative was circulating early enough to prompt an imperial response.

title: Gallio Inscription
description: A fragmentary inscription from Delphi mentioning Gallio as proconsul of Achaia, providing a crucial chronological anchor for Paul's ministry in Corinth.
significance: Allows precise dating of Paul's appearance before Gallio (Acts 18:12-17) to approximately AD 51-52, establishing a firm date in Pauline chronology.

title: Erastus Inscription
description: A Latin inscription found in Corinth reading 'Erastus, in return for his aedileship, laid this pavement at his own expense,' possibly the Erastus mentioned by Paul.
significance: May identify the city treasurer Erastus mentioned in Romans 16:23, providing a tangible connection between Paul's letters and the archaeological record of Corinth.

title: Politarch Inscription
description: Greek inscriptions from Thessalonica using the title 'politarch' (city official), a term once thought to be an error by Luke in the Book of Acts.
significance: Vindicates Luke's use of the term 'politarch' in Acts 17:6-8, demonstrating his precise knowledge of local governmental titles in Thessalonica.

title: Nebo-Sarsekim Tablet
description: A small Babylonian clay tablet mentioning Nebo-Sarsekim as chief eunuch of Nebuchadnezzar II, the same official named in Jeremiah 39:3.
significance: Confirms the historical accuracy of an otherwise obscure detail in Jeremiah, demonstrating the reliability of the prophet's account of the fall of Jerusalem.

title: LMLK Seal Impressions
description: Over 2,000 stamped jar handles bearing the Hebrew inscription 'LMLK' (belonging to the king), found throughout Judah and dating to Hezekiah's reign.
significance: Demonstrate the administrative organization of the Kingdom of Judah under Hezekiah, consistent with the biblical portrayal of his reign in 2 Kings 18-20.

title: Hezekiah's Royal Seal (Bulla)
description: A clay seal impression bearing the inscription 'Belonging to Hezekiah [son of] Ahaz, King of Judah,' discovered during excavations in Jerusalem.
significance: The first seal impression of an Israelite or Judean king found in a controlled archaeological excavation, directly confirming King Hezekiah's existence.

title: Isaiah Bulla
description: A clay seal impression found near the Hezekiah bulla reading 'Belonging to Isaiah,' possibly the prophet Isaiah who served during Hezekiah's reign.
significance: If confirmed as belonging to the prophet Isaiah, this would be the first extra-biblical evidence of the prophet, found mere feet from King Hezekiah's own seal.

title: Pontius Pilate Coin
description: Bronze prutah coins minted under Pontius Pilate as prefect of Judaea (AD 26-36), featuring a simpulum (ladle) and lituus (augur's wand) — Roman religious symbols.
significance: These coins are contemporaneous evidence of Pilate's governance of Judaea, supplementing the Pilate Inscription and literary sources about the man who sentenced Jesus.

title: Arch of Titus
description: A Roman triumphal arch in Rome depicting the spoils of the Jerusalem Temple being carried in procession after its destruction in AD 70.
significance: Provides visual confirmation of the destruction of the Second Temple, which Jesus prophesied in Matthew 24:2, Mark 13:2, and Luke 21:6.

title: Church of the Holy Sepulchre
description: An ancient church built over the traditional sites of Jesus' crucifixion (Golgotha) and burial, originally constructed by Emperor Constantine in the 4th century.
significance: Archaeological evidence confirms the site was a quarry and burial ground outside the city walls in the 1st century, consistent with the Gospel accounts of Jesus' crucifixion and burial.

title: Jericho Walls
description: Archaeological remains of massive fortification walls at Tell es-Sultan (ancient Jericho), showing evidence of destruction consistent with the biblical account.
significance: Excavations revealed collapsed mudbrick walls that fell outward (unusual for siege warfare), consistent with Joshua 6's account of Jericho's walls falling flat.

title: Gezer Calendar
description: A small limestone tablet with a Hebrew inscription listing agricultural activities by month, one of the oldest known Hebrew inscriptions (10th century BC).
significance: Demonstrates Hebrew literacy during the period of Solomon, when 1 Kings records that Gezer was given to Solomon as a wedding gift from Pharaoh (1 Kings 9:16).

title: Kurkh Monolith
description: An Assyrian monolith inscription describing the Battle of Qarqar (853 BC), listing 'Ahab the Israelite' as contributing 2,000 chariots and 10,000 soldiers.
significance: Provides extra-biblical evidence for King Ahab of Israel and his military strength, consistent with the portrayal of Ahab's reign in 1 Kings 16-22.

title: Ivory Pomegranate
description: A small ivory pomegranate with a partially preserved Hebrew inscription reading 'Belonging to the Temple of the Lord,' potentially from Solomon's Temple.
significance: If authentic, may be the only surviving artifact from Solomon's Temple. The pomegranate motif matches the Temple decorations described in 1 Kings 7:18-20.

title: Seal of Baruch ben Neriah
description: A clay seal impression reading 'Belonging to Berekhyahu, son of Neriyahu, the scribe' — identified as Baruch, the personal scribe of the prophet Jeremiah.
significance: Provides tangible evidence for the existence of Baruch, Jeremiah's scribe, mentioned in Jeremiah 32:12, 36:4, and 45:1.

title: Arad Ostraca
description: Over 100 inscribed pottery shards from the fortress of Arad in the Negev, including references to the 'House of YHWH' and priestly families mentioned in the Bible.
significance: Provide evidence of organized worship of YHWH and the administrative structure of Judah during the period of the monarchy described in Kings and Chronicles.

title: Ekron Inscription
description: A royal dedicatory inscription from Tel Miqne (ancient Ekron) mentioning five rulers of the city, confirming the identity of one of the five chief Philistine cities.
significance: Identifies ancient Ekron, one of the five Philistine cities mentioned repeatedly in the books of Joshua, Judges, and 1 Samuel.

title: Shishak Relief at Karnak
description: A large relief at the Temple of Karnak in Egypt depicting Pharaoh Sheshonq I's military campaign into Israel and Judah, listing over 150 conquered cities.
significance: Corroborates the biblical account of Pharaoh Shishak's invasion of Judah during Rehoboam's reign (1 Kings 14:25-26, 2 Chronicles 12:2-9).

title: City of David Excavations
description: Ongoing archaeological excavations in the oldest settled area of Jerusalem, revealing structures and artifacts from the biblical period including the Stepped Stone Structure.
significance: Reveals the physical reality of David's Jerusalem, including possible palatial structures from the 10th century BC consistent with the biblical account in 2 Samuel 5.

title: Elephantine Papyri
description: A collection of 5th-century BC papyrus documents from a Jewish military colony on Elephantine Island in Egypt, written in Aramaic.
significance: Provide evidence of Jewish diaspora communities during the Persian period, contemporary with the events of Ezra and Nehemiah, and mention the Temple in Jerusalem.

title: Sargon II Palace Inscriptions
description: Cuneiform inscriptions from Sargon II's palace at Khorsabad describing his conquest of Samaria and deportation of 27,290 Israelites in 722 BC.
current_location: Louvre Museum, Paris / Oriental Institute, Chicago
significance: Confirms the biblical account of the Assyrian conquest of the Northern Kingdom of Israel (2 Kings 17:6), providing the Assyrian perspective on this pivotal event.

title: Amarna Letters
description: A collection of 382 cuneiform tablets found in Egypt containing diplomatic correspondence between Egyptian pharaohs and Canaanite rulers (14th century BC).
significance: Mention the 'Habiru' people and describe conditions in Canaan shortly before or during the Israelite settlement period, providing context for the book of Joshua and Judges.

title: Magdala Stone
description: A carved stone block discovered in a 1st-century synagogue at Magdala (hometown of Mary Magdalene), featuring a unique seven-branched menorah carving.
significance: The synagogue at Magdala dates to the time of Jesus and may be one where he taught. The menorah carving is the earliest known stone depiction while the Temple still stood.

title: Crucified Man of Givat HaMivtar
description: Skeletal remains of a 1st-century man with a nail still embedded in his heel bone, providing the only direct archaeological evidence of Roman crucifixion.
significance: Provides physical evidence for the practice of crucifixion as described in the Gospel accounts of Jesus' death, confirming details about nail placement and burial practices.

title: Rylands Papyrus P52
description: A small fragment of papyrus containing verses from the Gospel of John (18:31-33, 37-38), dated to approximately AD 125, making it the earliest known New Testament manuscript.
significance: Demonstrates that the Gospel of John was in circulation in Egypt within a generation of its composition, supporting an early date for the writing of the Gospels.

title: Chester Beatty Papyri
description: A group of early Christian papyrus codices dating from the 2nd to 4th centuries, containing substantial portions of the Old and New Testaments in Greek.
current_location: Chester Beatty Library, Dublin / University of Michigan
significance: Among the oldest substantial manuscripts of the New Testament, predating Codex Sinaiticus by over a century. Include early copies of the Gospels, Acts, Pauline Epistles, and Revelation.

title: Capernaum Synagogue
description: Ruins of a limestone synagogue in Capernaum built over a 1st-century basalt foundation, at the site where Jesus established his Galilean ministry headquarters.
significance: The basalt foundation beneath the visible limestone structure dates to the 1st century, consistent with the synagogue where Jesus taught and performed miracles (Mark 1:21-28, Luke 4:31-37).

title: Seal of Jeroboam II
description: A jasper seal inscribed 'Belonging to Shema, servant of Jeroboam,' found at Megiddo, referencing a high official of King Jeroboam II of Israel (8th century BC).
significance: Provides direct epigraphic evidence for the reign of Jeroboam II, the powerful Israelite king described in 2 Kings 14:23-29 during whose reign the prophets Amos and Hosea ministered.
